sentence: The patient, a 30-year-old female, presents with a chronic history of asthma symptoms, including coughing and wheezing, which have been exacerbated by respiratory infections and exposure to her aunt's cat.
She reports frequent use of her albuterol inhaler, often needing to use it daily, and experiencing wheezing and coughing at night.
She has had several urgent care visits and one emergency department visit in the past year, often requiring prednisone treatment.
She denies any smoking or vaping history and has no history of seasonal allergies.
Vital signs: Blood pressure 128/82, respiratory rate 16, oxygen saturation 99% on room air.

Physical exam: Heart regular rate and rhythm, no ectopic beats, rubs, murmurs, or gallops; diminished lung sounds with occasional slight expiratory wheeze bilaterally; neck is supple, trachea midline, no lymphadenopathy, carotid bruit, clubbing, or capillary refill abnormality.
Assessment: The patient has moderate intermittent asthma, as evidenced by her symptoms and pulmonary function test results.
Problem list: Asthma; moderate intermittent asthma; suspected asthma exacerbation.
I prescribed Flovent 110 micrograms one puff twice per day to help prevent daily symptoms and reduce reliance on her albuterol inhaler.
I will also provide an asthma action plan to help manage her symptoms and identify triggers.
She is to continue using her Ventolin inhaler as needed for acute exacerbations, taking 2 puffs every 4-6 hours as needed.
I will see the patient in a month or sooner if she experiences worsening symptoms.
